coredata.dc:title: New lanthanide ternary complex system in electrospun nanofibers: Assembly, physico-chemical property and sensor application
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Xiangqian
Gu, Jiapei
Zhou, Zhan
Ma, Lufang
Tang, Yiping
Gao, Jinwei
Wang, Qianming
coredata.dc:description: The generation of novel optical sensing structures through the lanthanide coordination has been a key scientific endeavor. The design of molecular indicator for chemical response is derived from the assembly of a polymeric-based ternary europium (III) complex system (Eu(NTA)3L/PVA (NTA = 4,4,4-trifluoro-1-(naphthalen-2-yl)butane-1,3-dione, L = 2-(4-(4-(allyloxy)phenyl)-6-(pyridin-2-yl)pyridin-2-yl)pyridine, PVA = poly(vinyl acetate)). The electrospinning technique has been employed to create a nanometer scale membrane and this functional film with ultrafine fibers (diameter of about 110 nm) exhibits intense red light at 618 nm under ultra-violet excitation of 340 nm. The luminescent signal transduction involves the interaction of hydrogen chloride (HCl) with the organic ligand. The strong acidity of the analyte induces dissociation of complex network and the evolution of emission from red to blue has been observed. The fabricated nanosensor demonstrates rapid response time (<2 s) and the detection limit has been calculated to be 3.0 ppm. This effective strategy for achieving lanthanide complex-encapsulated polymer fibers will contribute to the development of new intelligent photonic devices.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lanthanide
Electrospinning
Film
Fluorescence

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Novel Materials for Energy and Advanced Applications

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Synthesis of the rare earth organic complex Eu(NTA)3
Synthesis of rare earth organic complex Eu(NTA)3L
Polymerization of precursors Eu(NTA)3L/PVA, L/PVA and PVA
Gas detection based on the Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Preparation of Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film and the detection of HCl gas.
UV–vis absorption spectra of ligand L, NTA, Eu(NTA)3 complex and Eu(NTA)3L complex (10 µM, H2O/DMSO = 2:1, v/v).
SEM image of Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film (A, B); Photographs of the Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film under daylight (C) and the exposure to UV excitation at 365 nm (D).
Emission spectra of Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film upon exposure to HCl vapor at room temperature (λex = 340 nm). (Inset: Excitation spectrum of Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film).

Reproducibility and reversibility of HCl gas sensing with Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film upon exposure to HCl/Et3N vapor at room temperature. (Inset: Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film upon exposure to Et3N (red) and HCl (blue) vapor and excited by UV light at 365 nm). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

Thermo-gravimetric analysis (TGA) curves of Eu(NTA)3L complex (Black) and Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film (Red). (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

head.ce:title: New lanthanide ternary complex system in electrospun nanofibers: Assembly, physico-chemical property and sensor application

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan Key Laboratory of Function-Oriented Porous Materials, Luoyang Normal University, Luoyang 471934, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering
Henan Key Laboratory of Function-Oriented Porous Materials

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry & Environment, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Material Science and Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou, Zhejiang, 310014, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center For Transparent Conductive Materials, South China Normal University, Guangzhou 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center For Transparent Conductive Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The generation of novel optical sensing structures through the lanthanide coordination has been a key scientific endeavor. The design of molecular indicator for chemical response is derived from the assembly of a polymeric-based ternary europium (III) complex system (Eu(NTA)3L/PVA (NTA = 4,4,4-trifluoro-1-(naphthalen-2-yl)butane-1,3-dione, L = 2-(4-(4-(allyloxy)phenyl)-6-(pyridin-2-yl)pyridin-2-yl)pyridine, PVA = poly(vinyl acetate)). The electrospinning technique has been employed to create a nanometer scale membrane and this functional film with ultrafine fibers (diameter of about 110 nm) exhibits intense red light at 618 nm under ultra-violet excitation of 340 nm. The luminescent signal transduction involves the interaction of hydrogen chloride (HCl) with the organic ligand. The strong acidity of the analyte induces dissociation of complex network and the evolution of emission from red to blue has been observed. The fabricated nanosensor demonstrates rapid response time (<2 s) and the detection limit has been calculated to be 3.0 ppm. This effective strategy for achieving lanthanide complex-encapsulated polymer fibers will contribute to the development of new intelligent photonic devices.

ce:section.ce:para: Chemical detection of guest species has generated considerable attention from last decade in terms of its availability and easy recognition. Hydrogen chloride (HCl) is a colorless, strongly acidic gas with an irritating odor. This active compound is highly toxic, and corrosive. It has been originated from burning fuels that contain chlorine (coal and incinerating waste that contains plastic) [1–3]. The threshold for its safe exposure is 10 ppm and the excessive amounts of HCl (35 ppm) can be hazardous to people’s health. For this reason, the concentration of HCl in the gas phase has been strictly controlled in many countries [4–7]. Therefore, it is a great interest to develop the new sensors for the monitoring of HCl gas to protect our safety as well as the environment. Currently, a great deal of efforts including amperometric [8], colorimetric [9] and solid electrochemical ways [10] have been undertaken to develop responsive sensors for hydrogen chloride gas sensing. Except the above mentioned approaches, fluorescence appears as one of the valuable transduction way to demonstrate the chemical recognition event. It will be a nondestructive examination for analytes and no reference will be necessary. Based on emissions, remote monitoring and different assay outputs (such as intensity, wavelength or lifetime) can be realized. Therefore, the fabrication of newly optical sensor with high stability, sensitivity, selectivity, good reversibility, and instrumental simplicity will be the challenging task in this field.

As for the selection of light sources, lanthanide elements possess quite a few unique features such as low self-absorption probability (large Stokes Shifts), line-like emission bands, long excited state lifetimes and excellent quantum efficiency. In recent years, trivalent lanthanide complexes have been explored as optical probes in sensing fields of anions, cations, and small molecules [11–25]. However, these series of complexes also suffer from several inherent shortcomings, such as low thermal-/photostability and poor processability, which would certainly restrict their practical applicability. In this range, a variety of new materials like graphene, Au nanoparticles, carbon quantum dots and silica networks were developed for the accommodation of lanthanide emitting species [26–34]. However, the most common and reliable way for the immobilization step will be the entrapment of the optical probe in a polymer matrix. This treatment is efficient due to the fact that the reactive system will be easily processable to nanoscale entities or thin films for sensor assembly. But the widths and sizes of the polymeric particles can be varied by changing the reaction time or experimental conditions. With the aim of improving the control of the shape, dimension or uniformity, electrospinning has been employed for the preparation of regular polymer materials with diameters in the range of nanometers [35–37]. So far as the microstructure is concerned, one-dimensional nanomaterials, especially nanofibers will arouse considerable interests due to their unique features. These membranes with fibrous microstructures have the superior feature for sensor fabrication since these nanobelts with growth directions possess much larger surface area compared with bulk films [38]. Moreover, the functional nanofibers containing lanthanide complexes will effectively improve mechanical strength and thermal stability of pure metal complexes. In addition, nanofibers assembled by electrospinning strategy exhibit excellent reproducibility and such large aspect ratio will open new opportunities for further exploration of the novel properties of such low-dimensional materials due to their extending surfaces.

Hence, we developed a new approach to rapid detection of hydrogen chloride gas based on a novel Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film via an effective fluorescence technology at room temperature (Fig. 1 ). At first, a novel organic building block (abbreviated as L) has been designed and synthesized. It owns pyridine rings to chelate lanthanide ions and the reactive group (CC bond) occurs polymerization with organic monomers. Secondly, the europium(III) complex (Eu(NTA)3L) has been assembled and co-polymerization is achieved between vinyl acetate (VA) and the metal complex. Finally, we used a one-step electrospun strategy to produce a flexible polymeric film. Its emission can be reversibly switched between blue and red upon exposure to HCl and Et3N vapor at room temperature with the rapid responses (2 s). As an alternative way for constructing organic-dye-impregnated hybrid materials, the incorporation of the lanthanide indicator in a polymeric solid support gains the following advantages: i) the extensive studies to detect strong acid gases were manly concentrated on the utilization of porphyrin and its derivatives. The employment of lanthanide forbidden transitions enabled us to develop the narrow emissions with long lifetimes for the establishment of new opto-chemical sensors; ii) Since the ionic size of lanthanide ions is usually large, its coordination modes are variable. In order to meet the requirement of high coordination number (9–12), the second ligand L has been encapsulated to increase its emission efficiency; iii) this polymeric-based sensor exhibited improved luminescent properties, thermal stability, and mechanical flexibility; iv) such cost-effective method (electrospinning treatment) was used for the fabrication of optical sensor and well-defined nanostructures were formed. To the best of our knowledge, the acid-sensing strategy for the generation of luminescent polymeric nanofibers containing ternary europium complex based on electrospinning technique has never been reported.

Eu(NO3)3·6H2O, p-Hydroxybenzaldehyde, 2-Acetylpyridine, allyl bromide, 4,4,4-trifluoro-1-(naphthalen-2-yl)butane-1,3-dione (NTA), vinyl acetate (VA) and 2,2′-Azobisisobutyronitrile were purchased from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). All the other chemicals were analytical grade reagents and used as received. All solvents, including methanol (CH3OH), ethanol (EtOH) and other metal salts of the highest purity available were obtained from Sigma-Aldrich Co. Ltd.

1H NMR spectra and high-resolution mass spectra were recorded on a Bruker Avance 600 MHz NMR Spectrometer. Scanning electron microscopy (SEM) was measured using a Tescan 5136MM scanning electron microscope. Elemental analyses were collected by Thermo Electron Flash EA112. FT-IR spectra was measured within the 4000–400 cm−1 region on an infrared spectrophotometer, Prestingge-21, using the KBr pellet technique. UV–Vis spectra were recorded on a TECHCOMP spectrophotometer in the range of 200–500 nm with a slit of 2 nm. Fluorescence and excitation spectra were measured using a Hitachi F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer with a 150 W xenon lamp as a light source. Absolute quantum yields were detected via an integrating sphere (Edinburgh FLS920) method. All the experiments were performed at ambient temperature.

p-Hydroxybenzaldehyde (244 mg, 2 mmol), 2-acetylpyridine (0.44 mL, 4 mmol) and potassium hydroxide (150 mg, 2 mmol) were added in ethanol (30 mL). After stirring 3 h at room temperature, ammonium hydroxide (2.9 mL, 2.5 mmol) was added and the solution was stirred for 8 h. The resulting precipitate was filtered and washed with ethanol to produce 4-(2,6-di(pyridin-2-yl)pyridin-4-yl)phenol as white powder in 60% yield. 4-(2,6-di(pyridin-2-yl)pyridin-4-yl)phenol (720 mg, 3 mmol), allyl bromide (0.96 mL, 12 mmol) and Et3N (1.38 mL, 12 mmol) were mixed in 60 mL of anhydrous CH2Cl2 at 0 °C. After stirring for 30 min, the mixture was allowed to react at room temperature for 1 h and then refluxed at 40 °C until the reaction was completed (monitored by TLC). The reaction mixture was diluted with CH2Cl2, washed with a saturated aqueous NaHCO3 solution, water and dried over anhydrous Na2SO4. The solvent was evaporated under reduced pressure. The crude solid product was purified by silica column chromatography (petroleum ether: ethyl acetate = 10/1, v/v) to afford the pure product L (Fig. S1). 1H NMR (600 MHz, d DMSO) δ 8.77–8.76 (d, J = 8.7 Hz, 2H), 8.69–8.66 (t, J = 12.0 Hz, 4H), 8.05–8.02 (d, J = 8.0 Hz, 2H), 7.90–7.89 (m, 2H), 7.54–7.52 (m, 2H), 7.17–7.16 (m,2H), 6.13–6.05 (d, J = 2.3 Hz, 1H), 5.46–5.44 (s, 1H), 5.31–5.30 (s, 1H), 4.80–4.67 (s, 2H) (Fig. S2). HR-MS (ESI): (C24H19N3O) m/z: calculated for [M + H]+: 365.2. Found [M + H]+: 366.1606 (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of the rare earth organic complex Eu(NTA)3
ce:section.ce:para: The Eu(NTA)3 complex was prepared by mixing ligand NTA (3.0 mmol, 798.66 mg) and Eu(NO3)3·6H2O (1.0 mmol, 446.07 mg) in ethanol. After the mixture was heated to 90 °C and refluxed for 4 h. The solvent was partially removed under reduced pressure and the resulting material was dried in a vacuum oven overnight to give the solid complex. Elemental analysis was carried out for Eu(NTA)3 (found (%): C, 44.05; H, 4.14; Eu, 32.69; F, 12.19; O, 6.93. Calc. (%): C, 43.98; H, 4.12; Eu, 32.73; F, 12.28; O, 6.89).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of rare earth organic complex Eu(NTA)3L
ce:section.ce:para: The Eu(NTA)3L complex was prepared by mixing ligand L (1.0 mmol, 410.2 mg) and Eu(NTA)3 (1.0 mmol, 950 mg) in ethanol. After the mixture was heated to 90 °C and refluxed for 4 h. The solvent was partially removed under reduced pressure and the resulting material was dried in a vacuum oven overnight to give the solid complex (Fig. 2 ). Elemental analysis was carried out for Eu(NTA)3L (found (%): C, 60.21; H, 5.02; Eu, 17.75; F, 6.71; N, 4.92; O, 5.39. Calc. (%): C, 60.14; H, 5.05; Eu, 17.70; F, 6.64; N, 4.89; O, 5.59).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Polymerization of precursors Eu(NTA)3L/PVA, L/PVA and PVA
ce:section.ce:para: The polymerization was performed under an atmosphere of nitrogen. Vinyl acetate (10 g, 0.116 mol) and Eu(NTA)3L (0.1 mmol, 136.0 mg) were placed into a three-necked round-bottom flask and was dissolved in methyl alcohol (10 mL). The mixed solution was purged with nitrogen for 30 min. AIBN (0.1 g, 0.61 mmol) was added and the reaction mixture was placed in a preheated oil bath at 65 °C for 12 h. The polymerization was quenched by cooling down the reaction mixture to room temperature and get Eu(NTA)3L/PVA polymer (Fig. 2). L/PVA polymer was synthesized in the similar procedure as Eu(NTA)3L/PVA, except for the use of ligand L (0.1 mmol, 41.0 mg). PVA polymer was synthesized in the similar procedure as Eu(NTA)3L/PVA without the use of ligand L and Eu(NTA)3L.

The Eu(NTA)3L/PVA polymer was dissolved in methanol and the sample was homogenized by stirring at 25 °C for 12 h prior to electrospinning process. Subsequently, the solution was poured into 10 mL plastic syringes with a 90° blunt stainless-steel needle with the inner diameter of 0.37 mm. A high positive voltage of 16 kV was applied to the needle to maintain the flow rate at 5.0 mL h−1 during electrospinning. The Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film was collected on grounded copper plate wrapped with aluminum foil over a gap distance of 15 cm and dried at room temperature for 12 h. L/PVA film was synthesized in the similar procedure as Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film, except for the use of precursors L/PVA polymer solution. PVA film was synthesized in the similar procedure as Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film, except for the use of PVA polymer solution as the precursor. Physical mixture of Eu(NTA)3L with PVA was prepared by doping Eu(NTA)3L together with PVA directly. Then the mixture was dispersed in methanol for electrospinning process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Gas detection based on the Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film
ce:section.ce:para: Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film was attached to a thin quartz slide with adhesive tape and placed into a quartz cuvette. The distance between the film and the cuvette bottom was set as 0.3 cm. Then it was exposed to HCl vapor. Mass flow controllers (0.1–10 mL/min, Horiba, Japan) were employed to achieve the desired concentration of HCl gas with dry nitrogen. The emission intensity was monitored from 350 to 670 nm with excitation wavelength fixed at 340 nm. In addition, the experimental setup for the detection of vapors was given in Fig. S4. (Cuvette size: length: 1 cm; width:1 cm; height: 2 cm).

The information of infrared radiation refers to the frequency of waves in a distance of one centimeter and it provides enough energy for chemical bonds in various forms of vibrations. Therefore, ligand L, NTA, Eu(NTA)3L complex, PVA film, and Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film were carried out for the investigation of the modification and coordination processes. As given in Fig. S5, the carbonyl symmetrical vibration bands of NTA (1603 cm−1) has been shifted to higher wavenumber in the Eu(NTA)3L complex (1614 cm−1), indicating that the coordination between oxygen atoms and Eu3+ ions would be formed. The absorption signals of Eu(NTA)3L complex at 1680 cm−1 might be caused by stretching vibration of CC bond, suggesting that ligand L would be involved in the coordinated structure of Eu(NTA)3L. During polymerization of Eu(NTA)3L complex and vinyl acetate, a one-step electrospun strategy was employed to prepare a robust Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film. For the comparison purpose, pure PVA film was also fabricated. The carbonyl symmetrical vibration band of PVA film (1732 cm−1) has been moved to high frequency range in Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film (1742 cm−1), indicating that the oxygen atoms in PVA moiety could also participate into the coordination with Eu3+ ions. The strong bands at 2977, 2936 and 2858 cm−1 might be corresponding to stretching vibrations of methylene spacer in grafted Eu(NTA)3L moiety. Characteristic band of vinyl units was absent in Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film and the results demonstrated that the unsaturated bonds would occur polymerization in the electrospinning process.

It is evident that measuring the ground-state radiation could offer valuable information concerning the substances. The absorption spectra of ligand L, NTA, Eu(NTA)3L complex are recorded by UV–Vis spectrometer. As shown in Fig. 3 , the ligand L (10 µM, H2O/DMSO = 2:1, v/v) displayed a broad absorption band in the UV range corresponding to conjugate system transitions with a maximum at 305 nm. The absorption spectra of NTA (10 µM, H2O/DMSO = 2:1, v/v) gave rise to a series of peaks at 250, 290 and 335 nm, which can be attributed to the π–π* transitions. In the curve of Eu(NTA)3L complex, the ligand-centered absorption bands exhibited red shifts for the peak wavelengths. Compared with Eu(NTA)3 complex (10 µM, H2O/DMSO = 2:1, v/v), the absorption peak of ternary complex (Eu(NTA)3L) was centered at around 335 nm and gave rise to a bathochromic shift. The fact would support the coordination interaction with ligand L and the incorporation of such terpyridine derivative (ligand L) might enlarge the conjugation system. Furthermore, the overall signal of Eu(NTA)3L (10 µM, H2O/DMSO = 2:1, v/v) at room temperature also covered a wide range of wavelengths in contrast to pure L and NTA molecule. The tail of the band could extend into visible light area (near to 400 nm). It was mainly induced by the polarization of the Eu3+ ion after the coordination, which resulted in the ligand-to-metal charge transfer, thereby the energy gap has been reduced.

The morphologies of Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film were investigated by using SEM (Fig. 4 A and B). The SEM images show that the average diameter of Eu(NTA)3L/PVA nanofibers would be around 110 nm. Their surfaces were quite smooth and the uniform nanostructures indicated that the microstructure and size could be finely controlled by the spinning technique. As shown in Fig. S6, the thickness of Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film is approximately 40.5 µm. With the aim of exploring potential applications, we further studied the emission properties of Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film. In Fig. 4C, the film was homogenous and in a good shape. Under the excitation by a ultra-violet lamp irradiation at 365 nm, the functional film possessed unique optical properties and exhibited bright red fluorescence. As shown in Fig. S7, the excitation spectrum was consisted of a broad band covering from 320 to 390 nm with a peak maximum at 340 nm when monitoring emission wavelength at 618 nm. The solid-state emission spectra of Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film revealed that the typical transitions from 5D0 → 7FJ (J = 0, 1, 2, 3) of the Eu3+ ions at 581, 596, 618 and 655 nm, respectively. The involvement of PVA moiety in the coordination structure has been clarified by further photophysical studies. The lifetimes and quantum yields of both Eu(NTA)3L complex and Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film were measured (Figs. S8, S9 and Table S1). The decay curves could be fitted by double-exponential profiles with the lifetimes of ∼1.38 ms, ∼0.79 ms (Eu(NTA)3L) and ∼1.47 ms, ∼0.75 ms (Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film). Compared with free Eu(NTA)3L complex, the average lifetime and quantum efficiency of Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film increased from 1.26 ms to 1.35 ms and from 9.8% to 24.6%, respectively. These results provided useful information that PVA would take part in the coordination framework.

It is well accepted that the microenvironment of rare earth ions is delicate and sensitive to external stimuli [39,40]. The elucidation of luminescence change will be closely related to guest sensing. Interestingly, the emission features varied significantly and the fluorescence peak evolved from red region (centered at 618 nm) to blue region (centered at 453 nm) upon exposure to HCl vapor at room temperature. A large peak shift (165 nm) and emitting color change would be achieved (Figs. 5 and S10). It has been found that the correlation between the intensity ratio (I453/I618) and the concentration of HCl vapor follows a linear equation in the range of 5 to 100 ppm (Y = 0.009 X + 0.094 (R2 = 0.998)). The detection limit has been determined to be 3.0 ppm (Fig. S11) via the equation DL = 3 × SD/slope, where SD is the standard deviation of the blank sample [41,42]. Parallel experiments were carried out to ensure the accuracy and precision of the proposed method and the relative standard deviation was calculated to be 0.9%.

A general comparison with reported literatures was given in Table S2. We have to admit that the detection limit in this research was not the lowest one, but such functional film owns two advantages. Firstly, the employment of forbidden transitions from lanthanide ions (long-lived emission) would be readily distinguished from most of short-lived noisy fluorescent signals. Secondly, the responsive time (2 s) here was very efficient and would be considered to be an attractive feature from a recognition viewpoint. For the sake of confirming the sensing response during the recognition process, we have measured the emission intensity changes at 618 nm (Fig. S12). Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film was exposed to HCl vapor depending on duration time. The results gave rise to fast response time (2 s) in the presence of HCl gas.

We also studied the selectivity performance of the film sensor, the emission changes (I453/I618) of Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film were recorded upon exposure to HCl vapor and other analogous analytes, including HNO3, H2SO4, CO2, H2S, SO2, NO2 and NH3. As is given in Fig. S13, a significant increase of I453/I618 could be observed when the sensor was exposed to HCl gas. Under similar conditions, Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film showed almost no responses to H2SO4, CO2, H2S, SO2, NO2 or NH3. It was also found that HNO3 could cause the signal changes. In order to clearly explore the difference of Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film upon exposure to HCl or HNO3 gas, we collected the fluorescent titration spectra of Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film toward HNO3 gas. It was observed that the correlation between the intensity ratio (I453/I618) and the concentration of HNO3 vapor follows a linear equation (Y = 0.003 X − 0.160 (R2 = 0.991)) from 60 to 250 ppm (Fig. S14). The detection limit was determined to be 27 ppm and such value was higher than that of HCl. The relative standard deviation was calculated to be 2.7%. In this way, it was believed that Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film owns enough optical selectivity for HCl.

It is known that lanthanide complex has been generally formed at neutral or alkaline conditions, the protons especially the strong acid will affect the complex structure. In this study, Eu(NTA)3L complex would be dissociated at acidic conditions and the absorbed energy of ligand L cannot be transferred to the europium ions. In this case, the red emission was suppressed and the original blue emission of the ligand L was recovered. To further study this sensing mechanism, we assembled ligand L grafted PVA film as a control experiment. As provided in Fig. S15, it has been found that this composite film had a maximum peak at 450 nm, which was in agreement with the results of Fig. 5 . This fact confirmed the previous photophysical studies and the blue emission of Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film upon exposure to HCl vapor would be derived from the luminescence of ligand L. The current data supported that Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film can be used as a responsive probe for the sensitive detection of HCl gas. In order to further clarify the chemical change during the recognition process, the ultra-violet absorption spectra in the presence of HCl or Et3N were recorded (Fig. S16). Upon exposure to HCl gas, the broad band at 335 nm was reduced substantially, indicating that the coordination between the ligand and europium ions would be ruined within the acidic environment. In the presence of Et3N, the absorption signals were recovered.

If the fluorescence signal of the nano-probe could be reversible, the promising uses in real-time HCl gas monitoring will be more significant. As shown in Fig. 6 , it is obvious that the emission intensity of the sensor remained nearly unchanged after five cycles of tracing and recovery, which supported that the sensor owned very excellent reversibility. This recycle process has been further proved by the emission curve changes in Fig. S17 and the repetitive usage for the detection of HCl would be realized. We have also explored the microstructure of Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film upon exposure to HCl/Et3N vapor for five cycles. As presented in Fig. S18, it gave rise to no apparent changes compared with original Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film, the results revealed that the morphology was not affected by the presence of gases and such polymeric-based sensor possessed enough stability.

Photo-stability will be the key property to stimulate practical application in a variety of fields. We explored emission signals of Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film at different time durations. As for the Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film, the reduction in emission intensities at 618 nm were less than 5% within 30 days (Fig. S19). The collected results demonstrated that the photoluminescence intensity could be maintained within a relatively longer time. Upon the exposure to the 365 nm ultra-violet lamp irradiation for 120 mins at room temperature in ambient air, the emission intensity was still approximately equal to 95% of the initial intensity, indicating the electrospinning film had outstanding photostability for future uses (Fig. S20). It was also found that the time-dependence stability and photo-stability of pure complex (Eu(NTA)3L) were lower than Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film. The polymeric network shielded the lanthanide complex from external influences. The dissociation of metal complex would be effectively avoided in the solid-state host and the emissive species were anchored onto specific location due to synergistic effects between complex and polymer.

It should be emphasized that the functional polymeric material with covalent bond in this work (Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film) was prepared by the co-polymerization of lanthanide complex containing monomer and organic monomer. The resultant europium complex units would be uniformly bonded to polymeric network. But the simple physical mixture (doping Eu(NTA)3L with PVA) would cause serious concentration quenching due to the occurrence of aggregates [43]. The photoluminescence measurements of the two materials showed that Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film gained advantage over the physical mixture (Fig. S21) and the luminescence intensities were found to depend on synthesis strategy.

Thermo-gravimetric analysis has been used to investigate the thermal stability and decomposition process of chemicals and materials. In order to further clarify the thermal features of Eu(NTA)3L complex and covalent hybrid Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film, the TG analysis was also performed. As provided in Fig. 7 , it has been found that the covalent bond polymeric film possessed extraordinary thermal stability. In the range of 25–200 °C, the weight loss could be attributed to the removal of adsorbed water and coordination water molecules in the Eu(NTA)3L complex. Over 200 °C, the major weight loss may be generated by the collapse of the coordination structures and the thermal degradation of the organic components. The results revealed that the Eu(NTA)3L complex had a great weight loss over 200 °C while the weight of the covalent hybrid Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film decreased slightly in the temperature range from 25 to 300 °C. It is estimated that the Eu(NTA)3L complex has been covalently bonded to nanofibers, the effective cross-linking of vinyl bonds will largely restrict the mobility of europium complex and the decomposition temperature was improved. In this way, the covalent-bonded hybrid Eu(NTA)3L/PVA film demonstrated a remarkable increase in thermal property and the new doorway for the preparation of photonic devices could be opened.

In summary, the covalent attachment of organic building block (2-(4-(4-(allyloxy)phenyl)-6-(pyridin-2-yl)pyridin-2-yl)pyridine, L) onto the polymeric chains and the co-immobilization of lanthanide complex with poly(vinyl acetate) have been reported. The regular and uniform fibrous structures were achieved by using an electrospinning deposition technique. Emission color from europium characteristic peaks were also identified. Additionally, the feasibility of the smart nanofibers as potential sensor materials was realized by sharp luminescence change from red to blue upon protonation of acidity. The superior thermal stability of such nanofibrous membrane will be valuable for monitoring HCl gas release in practical environment.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Q. M. acknowledges the support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (grants number 21371063) and Guangdong Science and Technology plan (2016A050502053). Z. Z. acknowledges the Scientific Research Fund of Henan Provincial Education Department (17A150016) and Natural Science Foundation of Henan (162300410200) for financial assistances. J.W. thanks for the support from Innovation team project by department of Education of Guangdong Province (2016KCXTD009).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2018.10.003.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
